lesson.menu_title: When Things Go Wrong -- Misunderstandings, Clarification, and the Unexpected
lesson.menu_desc: Stay in English when a trip goes wrong. Read the story of a day in Seoul when the reservation was lost, the flight was cancelled, and the badge was wrong -- and how each problem was solved, one polite question at a time. The class trains polite requests (Could you check that again? / Would it be possible to change the room?) and apologies for problems (I'm sorry for the delay. / I apologize for the misunderstanding.), plus clarification and buy-time chunks (Just to make sure... / Bear with me.). Vocabulary: mistake, delay, cancel, apologize, solve, replace, misunderstanding, reservation. Expression: 'Just to make sure...' Idiom: 'Bear with me.' -- 33 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 18 -- When Things Go Wrong: Clarification and the Unexpected
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Maria Clara | Lesson 18 -- When Things Go Wrong: Clarification and the Unexpected
lesson.grammar_point: polite requests and apologies for problems

listenings.text: Hi Maria Clara, it is Bruno. I am calling before you land in Seoul, so please listen carefully. There is a small problem, but do not worry. First, the hotel says there might be a mistake with your reservation, because the date is wrong. If your name is not on the list, stay calm and ask them to check again. Second, the interpreter's flight is delayed, and it might be cancelled, so you may be alone at the conference desk for one day. And one more thing: last time they gave you the wrong badge, so just to make sure, check the session on your badge. You know what to do. Ask, apologize if you need to, and solve it one question at a time. Bear with them, and they will bear with you. Call me if you need anything. See you soon.

Let me tell you about my first day in Seoul. Almost everything went wrong. My reservation was not on the list. The interpreter's flight was cancelled. And they gave me the wrong badge for the wrong session. Last year, I know what I would have done. I would have switched to Portuguese, and gone quiet. But not this time. I took a breath and I said, I think there might be a mistake, could you check again, please? When I did not understand, I said, I'm sorry, could you say that again? When I needed a moment, I said, bear with me. Nothing was perfect. But I solved every problem, one polite question at a time. I did not need perfect English. I needed calm English. And this time, I stayed. I stayed in English, and I spoke for my institution myself.

reading.rtitle: A Day When Everything Went Wrong
paras: Maria Clara arrives at her hotel in Seoul after a long flight. She is tired, but calm. At the front desk, the receptionist looks at the computer and says her name is not on the list. There is a problem with her reservation. For a second, she wants to switch to Portuguese. Instead, she takes a breath and says, "I think there might be a mistake. Could you check again, please?"

The receptionist checks again. The reservation is there, but the date is wrong. "I'm sorry for the misunderstanding," the receptionist says. Maria Clara does not panic. "Bear with me," she thinks, and asks slowly, "Would it be possible to give me a room for tonight?" The receptionist smiles and finds a room. The first problem is solved.

The next morning, there is more bad news. The interpreter's flight is delayed, and then the airline cancels it. At the conference desk, a coordinator gives her the wrong badge, for the wrong session. It is a small misunderstanding, but it matters. "Just to make sure," she says, "could you replace this badge? I think there is a mistake with the session."

By the end of the day, nothing went as planned: a wrong reservation, a cancelled flight, a wrong badge. But Maria Clara did not disappear into silence or Portuguese. She apologized when she needed to, she asked for help in clear English, and she solved every problem, one polite question at a time. "When things go wrong," she writes in her notebook, "I do not need perfect English. I need calm English."
reading.source: Institutional & Travel English -- narrative written for Maria Clara's package

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of this text?
choices: Maria Clara cancels her trip to Seoul because too many things went wrong.
Maria Clara stays calm and solves several unexpected problems in polite English, instead of switching to Portuguese.
Maria Clara complains to the hotel manager and asks for her money back.

items: At first, Maria Clara's name was not on the hotel list.
t
"the receptionist ... says her name is not on the list. There is a problem with her reservation."
When there was a problem, Maria Clara switched to Portuguese.
f
"Maria Clara did not disappear into silence or Portuguese."
The interpreter's flight was delayed and then cancelled.
t
"The interpreter's flight is delayed, and then the airline cancels it."
The coordinator gave her the correct badge for the right session.
f
"a coordinator gives her the wrong badge, for the wrong session."
Maria Clara solved the problems with polite questions.
t
"she solved every problem, one polite question at a time."

bank.label: Asking politely, clarifying, and apologizing when things go wrong
items: I think there might be a mistake. Could you check again, please?
I'm sorry -- could you say that again?
Would it be possible to change the room?
Just to make sure, could you repeat that?
I'm sorry for the delay. My flight was cancelled.
Bear with me -- I want to say this correctly.

items.situation: You arrive at the hotel and your reservation is not on the list. Ask the receptionist to check again, politely.
tips: I think there might be a mistake. Could you check again, please?
Could you + verb...? = a polite request.

items.situation: The coordinator speaks very fast and you do not understand. Ask them to slow down or repeat.
tips: I'm sorry -- could you speak more slowly?

items.situation: You get the wrong badge for the wrong session. Ask them to replace it.
tips: Just to make sure -- could you replace this badge?
There is a mistake with the session.

items.situation: Your flight is delayed and you will arrive late. Apologize and explain.
tips: I'm sorry for the delay. My flight was cancelled.

items.situation: You need a moment to find the English words. Ask the person to wait.
tips: Just to make sure I understand...

items.situation: You want to change your hotel room. Ask if it is possible, politely.
tips: Would it be possible to change the room?
Would it be possible to + verb...?

pressure.title: Answer live
items: Something is wrong with your reservation. In one sentence, ask the receptionist to check again -- politely. Go.
The coordinator is speaking too fast. Ask them, in two different ways, to slow down or repeat. Do not stop.
Tell me about a real moment when your English went wrong. What will you say next time, in English, to stay calm? Thirty seconds.

pressure.text: Polite request: Could you + base verb...? (Could you check that again?) and Would it be possible to + base verb...? (Would it be possible to change the room?). Apology: I'm sorry for + noun / I apologize for + noun (I'm sorry for the delay). Clarify: Could you repeat that? / Could you say that again? / Could you speak more slowly? Buy time: Just to make sure... / Bear with me.
